stem: A 6-week-old, ex-39-week, formula-fed, vaccinated-to-age boy is brought to ED by his mother after a measured rectal temperature of 38.4 deg C at home this morning. He has fed slightly less than usual for 6 hours but has had wet nappies, no vomiting, no diarrhoea, no rash and no cough. Maternal GBS negative at 36 weeks, membranes ruptured 4 hours, discharged day 2 without antibiotics. He was previously well. On arrival he is alert, settles to feed, central capillary refill 2 seconds, no focus on examination (clear chest, soft abdomen, flat fontanelle, clean umbilicus). Recorded temperature in ED is 38.2 deg C. Initial bloods: WCC 9.8, neutrophils 5.2, lymphocytes 3.6, CRP 8. Catheter urinalysis: nitrites negative, leukocytes negative. T 38.2, HR 148, RR 42, SpO2 99 percent. The ED registrar is deciding whether lumbar puncture is required.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2 degC, HR 148 bpm, RR 42, SpO2 99% RA, cap refill 2 s.
data_table.Examination: Alert, feeds well. No rash. Fontanelle flat. Chest clear. Abdomen soft. Umbilicus clean.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 9.8, neutrophils 5.2, lymphocytes 3.6, CRP 8 mg/L. Urine dip negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and open 24-hour GP review
options.rationale: Outpatient management without workup is unsafe at this age; the under-3-months febrile-infant rule overrides reassuring appearance. Would be acceptable only if the child were over 3 months, well, and meeting NICE green-zone criteria (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).

options.text: Blood and urine cultures (catheter), lumbar puncture, then commence IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime; admit
options.rationale: The AU age-cut-off for mandatory full septic workup INCLUDING lumbar puncture is under 8 weeks of age (about 56 days) regardless of clinical appearance because low-risk criteria do not exclude bacterial meningitis at this age. At 6 weeks the recommended empirical regimen is benzylpenicillin/ampicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime (Listeria, Gram-negatives, CNS penetration), per RCH CPG.

options.text: Blood and urine cultures only and observe without antibiotics pending results
options.rationale: Omitting LP is the classic anti-pattern in under-8-week infants: low-risk biomarkers do not exclude meningitis. Selective workup without LP applies only to well 8-week to 3-month infants meeting all Rochester/Philadelphia low-risk criteria (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin and ED review at 24 hours
options.rationale: PO amoxicillin gives inadequate CSF penetration and serum levels for neonatal sepsis or meningitis; oral therapy is never empirical cover in suspected SBI under 3 months. Would be reasonable only for a confirmed mild outpatient bacterial focus (e.g. acute otitis media) in an older well child (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Chest x-ray and 4 hours observation without further investigation
options.rationale: CXR is a focus-driven investigation, not a sepsis screen, and substitutes for none of the required cultures. Would be indicated only with respiratory signs (tachypnoea out of proportion, focal crackles, hypoxia) (Source: NICE NG143).

explanation.summary: Febrile infants under 8 weeks (some Australian centres extend to under 12 weeks) require full septic workup including CSF and empirical IV antibiotics regardless of low-risk biomarkers, because the Rochester, Philadelphia and Boston low-risk criteria miss approximately 1-3% of serious bacterial infections including meningitis.
key_points: Under 8 weeks: full septic workup including LP, empirical IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin
8 weeks to 3 months: selective workup possible if well AND low-risk criteria met
Rochester low-risk: WCC 5-15, bands <1.5, urine WBC <10, stool WBC <5 if diarrhoea
CRP >20 and procalcitonin >0.5 increase risk of SBI
Listeria cover (amoxicillin/benzylpenicillin) required under 3 months
explanation.pearls: A well-looking febrile neonate is the most dangerous febrile patient in the department. Bacterial meningitis can present with normal WCC and CRP at this age.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Febrile child
Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Sepsis in neonates and infants

subtopic_detail: Febrile young infant - septic workup criteria

stem: A 3-year-old girl, weight 14 kg, presents to a regional ED at 02:30 with 18 hours of fever to 39.4 degC, reduced oral intake and increasing lethargy. She had a coryzal illness in the family last week. Past history includes mild eczema only and her vaccinations are up to date. On triage she is sleepy but rousable to voice, with mottled peripheries to the knees and elbows, cool hands and feet, a central capillary refill of 4 seconds, and a non-blanching petechial rash spreading on the trunk. She has not passed urine since the previous evening. Temperature is 39.1 degC, heart rate 178 bpm, respiratory rate 44, SpO2 96% on room air, and non-invasive BP is 92/58 mmHg. She does not localise to pain on abdominal examination. The nurse has activated a Paediatric Early Warning Score escalation and IV access has been obtained.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1 degC, HR 178 bpm, RR 44, SpO2 96% RA, BP 92/58, cap refill 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Sleepy, rousable. Mottled to elbows/knees. Non-blanching petechial rash trunk. No neck stiffness elicited.
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.28, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, glucose 4.1 mmol/L, BE -7.

lead_in: Which set of interventions best meets the paediatric sepsis 1-hour bundle?

options.text: IV adrenaline infusion as first intervention
options.rationale: Adrenaline before adequate volume worsens outcomes by increasing afterload on an under-filled ventricle. Would be correct only after 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid in fluid-refractory shock, and even then peripheral adrenaline 0.05-0.1 microg/kg/min is started while central access is obtained (Source: ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis Pathway).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin, 10 mL/kg saline bolus, then urgent CT head
options.rationale: Three errors stacked: oral antibiotic in shock, sub-therapeutic 10 mL/kg bolus, and imaging-before-antibiotics. CT head is not required prior to antibiotics even with suspected meningitis; antibiotics are given first and CT/LP follow once stable (Source: ANZICS; RCH CPG Meningitis).

options.text: Blood cultures, IV ceftriaxone, 20 mL/kg saline bolus, lactate, then reassess
options.rationale: The Australian paediatric sepsis pathway requires recognition, IV/IO access, blood cultures before antibiotics where this does not delay, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour (ceftriaxone covers meningococcaemia which is the likely diagnosis given non-blanching rash and shock), a 10-20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid bolus with reassessment, and lactate measurement - all within the first hour (ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis Pathway; RCH CPG Sepsis).

options.text: Paracetamol PO and observation pending senior review
options.rationale: Treating meningococcal shock as a viral fever is the cardinal sepsis miss. Antipyretic-and-observe would only be appropriate for a well-appearing child with a clear viral focus and no red flags (Source: ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis Pathway).

options.text: Lumbar puncture first, then IV antibiotics, then fluid bolus over 15 minutes
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated in shock or raised ICP and must never delay antibiotics. LP-first would be acceptable only in a stable well-perfused child with isolated meningism, normal GCS and no coagulopathy (Source: RCH CPG: Meningitis).

explanation.summary: Recognise paediatric sepsis from tachycardia disproportionate to fever, altered mental status, prolonged capillary refill and mottling - hypotension is a late sign. Deliver the 1-hour bundle: oxygen, IV/IO access, blood cultures, broad-spectrum IV antibiotic, 10-20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid bolus with reassessment after each, lactate, and senior/PIPER escalation.
key_points: Cold shock: cool peripheries, prolonged CR, narrow pulse pressure - common in children
Warm shock: bounding pulses, flash CR, wide pulse pressure - older children/adolescents
Antibiotic within 1 hour; do not delay for LP or imaging
Reassess after each 10-20 mL/kg bolus; watch for fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles)
Escalate to PIPER/NETS for transfer to PICU
explanation.pearls: A child with sepsis decompensates suddenly because they compensate well right up to the cliff edge. Trust prolonged capillary refill and altered mentation over a normal BP.

stem: An 18-month-old boy weighing 11 kg (clinic weight 2 weeks ago 11.5 kg) presents with 36 hours of profuse non-bloody diarrhoea and three episodes of vomiting today. His older sibling had similar symptoms last week. He has been drinking some water but refuses solids. On examination he is irritable but consolable, with sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, mildly delayed skin recoil, central capillary refill 2-3 seconds, warm peripheries and tears with crying. Triage nappy was dry; the last wet nappy was 6 hours ago. HR 142, RR 28, T 37.4 deg C, BP 96/60. There is no abdominal mass and no peritonism.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 degC, HR 142 bpm, RR 28, BP 96/60, cap refill 2-3 s.
data_table.Examination: Irritable. Sunken eyes, dry mucosae. Tears present. Skin recoil mildly delayed. Warm peripheries.
data_table.Investigations: Weight 11 kg (4% below recent baseline 11.5 kg). Urine output reduced.

lead_in: Which combination of dehydration assessment and immediate rehydration plan is most appropriate?

options.text: No clinically detectable dehydration (under 3 percent); oral fluids ad lib at home without need for any structured rehydration regimen today
options.rationale: Multiple Gorelick signs and a measured weight deficit are present, so dehydration is clinically detectable. Under 3 percent would require fewer than 2 Gorelick signs, no measured weight loss, and intact tears, mucosae, and skin turgor (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).

options.text: Mild to moderate dehydration ~4-6% (3-5 Gorelick signs, preserved consciousness/tears/BP, weight deficit 0.5/11.5 kg = ~4.3%); commence NGT or ORS at 50 mL/kg over 4 hours plus losses
options.rationale: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis classification uses the Gorelick signs (sunken eyes, dry mucosae, delayed skin recoil, reduced UO, mildly prolonged CR) and weight deficit. Mild-moderate dehydration around 4-6 percent is managed with nasogastric ORS at 50 mL/kg over 4 hours (or oral if tolerating), with IV reserved for failure of NG or shock.

options.text: Severe dehydration (at least 10%) with compensated shock; resuscitate with 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline boluses repeated as needed in the resuscitation bay
options.rationale: Severe (10 percent plus) dehydration requires obtundation, prolonged central CR over 3 seconds, weak pulses and absent tears; over-classifying triggers unnecessary IV resuscitation. Bolus saline would be correct only with shock features or true severe deficit (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).

options.text: Decompensated hypovolaemic shock from severe dehydration; intubate and resuscitate with rapid sequence induction and large-volume isotonic boluses now
options.rationale: Intubation for hypovolaemic shock is reserved for failed resuscitation with persistent altered consciousness or airway compromise; this child has preserved BP, GCS and central perfusion. Decompensated shock would require absent peripheral pulses and altered mental state (Source: APLS).

options.text: Euvolaemic viral illness; reassure and discharge on oral fluids ad libitum with GP review in 24 hours and no structured rehydration regimen today
options.rationale: Measured weight loss and clinical signs prove a deficit; calling this euvolaemic and discharging is the classic anti-pattern of trusting appearance over weight. Reassure-only is acceptable only when serial weights and clinical exam are both normal (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).

explanation.summary: Use the Gorelick 10-point or WHO 4-sign assessment together with acute weight loss. Mild-moderate dehydration (3-7%) has 2-3 signs and preserved consciousness, tears and BP. Severe (>=10%) has obtunded mental state, deeply sunken eyes, absent tears, very prolonged CR and weak pulses.
key_points: Best single estimate of deficit: acute weight change
Most useful clinical signs: prolonged CR, abnormal skin turgor, abnormal respiratory pattern
BP is a late sign in children - compensated shock can have a normal BP
WHO: no/some/severe dehydration plans A/B/C
Gorelick >=3 signs = >5% dehydration
explanation.pearls: If the child is alert, drinking, and producing tears, severe dehydration is unlikely no matter how dramatic the diarrhoea history.

stem: A 2-year-old girl, weight 12 kg, presents with 48 hours of viral gastroenteritis (vomiting and watery diarrhoea, no blood). She is assessed in ED as approximately 5-6% dehydrated: dry mucosae, sunken eyes, capillary refill 2 s, reduced urine output. She is alert and accepts sips of water but vomits a 30 mL bolus after 5 minutes. She has no ileus, no abdominal distension and no surgical concerns. Electrolytes are not yet available. She has no significant comorbidity, no allergies and weighs near her plotted growth centile. The ED is busy and IV cannulation has been attempted unsuccessfully once. Her parents are willing to assist with whatever route is recommended. She has been in family day care this week where two other children have had similar symptoms. Her mother reports approximately 6-8 large watery stools per day and increasing thirst, and she has been refusing the Gastrolyte sachets the GP recommended yesterday because of taste. She has no underlying renal, cardiac or metabolic disease and was born at term with no neonatal complications.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 degC, HR 138, RR 28, BP 98/62, cap refill 2 s.
data_table.Examination: Alert, irritable. Dry mucosae. Soft abdomen, no distension, no peritonism. Bowel sounds active.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.4 mmol/L. No electrolytes yet.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate rehydration approach?

options.text: IV 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg bolus then deficit IV over 24 hours
options.rationale: IV is reserved for shock, severe dehydration, ileus, altered mental state or failed enteral therapy; jumping to IV in mild-moderate dehydration is the AU anti-pattern when NG ORS is well validated. Would be correct with shock or failed NG trial (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).

options.text: IV 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose at maintenance rate only, no bolus
options.rationale: 0.45 percent saline is no longer recommended as routine paediatric IV maintenance in Australia due to hospital-acquired hyponatraemia. Isotonic fluid (Plasma-Lyte or 0.9 percent saline with 5 percent dextrose) is standard if IV is required (Source: NHMRC Paediatric IV Fluids; RCH CPG IV Fluids).

options.text: Water and dilute apple juice ad libitum with GP review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Plain water and dilute juice lack adequate sodium and glucose, risking hyponatraemia in ongoing losses; this is the home-remedy anti-pattern. ORS or commercial hydration solutions are required, and ED rehydration is needed given failed oral intake (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).

options.text: Nasogastric oral rehydration solution at 25 mL/kg/hour for 4 hours then reassess
options.rationale: Rapid NG rehydration with ORS (Gastrolyte) at 25 mL/kg/h for 4 hours is the RCH first-line approach for mild-moderate dehydration in children who cannot tolerate oral sips, avoiding the risks of IV access and being non-inferior to IV for time to discharge and complications (RCH CPG Gastroenteritis; PREDICT NG/IV trial).

options.text: Single ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg IV then discharge without any rehydration
options.rationale: Ondansetron enables ORS but does not replace it; single-dose-and-discharge without verified intake misses ongoing deficit. Would be appropriate only as an adjunct before a documented successful oral trial (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis).

explanation.summary: Mild-moderate dehydration (3-7%) is first managed enterally. Trial oral ORS in small frequent volumes (e.g., 5 mL every 1-2 minutes); if vomiting, give ondansetron (>=6 months) and re-trial, or switch to rapid NG rehydration at 25 mL/kg/h for 4 hours then reassess. IV is reserved for shock, severe dehydration, ileus, altered consciousness or failed enteral rehydration. Use isotonic fluids if IV is required.
key_points: Enteral first: oral then NG; both better tolerated than IV and equally effective in mild-moderate
Rapid NG: 25 mL/kg/h ORS for 4 hours = 100 mL/kg replacement
Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV reduces vomiting; one dose only
IV bolus 10-20 mL/kg 0.9% saline for shock; isotonic maintenance
Avoid hypotonic IV fluids - risk of hyponatraemic encephalopathy
explanation.pearls: An NG tube placed for rehydration is rarely refused once parents understand it avoids repeat cannulation attempts.

sources.label: PREDICT NG vs IV rehydration trial

stem: A 5-year-old boy weighing 20 kg is brought to a school sick bay 10 minutes after eating a peanut butter sandwich. He had a mild rash to peanut at age 2. Staff describe him as flushed with widespread urticaria, persistent coughing with audible wheeze, hoarse voice and reported throat tightness. He is anxious but responsive, sitting forward. He has no past history of asthma. HR 138, RR 38, SpO2 94 percent on room air. There is no stridor at rest. His EpiPen Jr 150 microgram is in the school office and an ambulance has been called. He has not received any treatment yet.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 degC, HR 138 bpm, RR 38, SpO2 94% RA.
data_table.Examination: Flushed, widespread urticaria. Hoarse voice, throat tightness, audible wheeze. Sitting forward. No stridor at rest.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate action, including correct adrenaline dose for his weight?

options.text: Lay supine with legs elevated unless respiratory distress; IM adrenaline 150 microgram (EpiPen Jr) anterolateral mid-thigh - he weighs 20 kg, the ASCIA 10-20 kg threshold
options.rationale: He meets ASCIA criteria for anaphylaxis (skin plus respiratory involvement with airway compromise). IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg) is first-line. ASCIA autoinjector thresholds: 10-20 kg use 150 microgram (EpiPen Jr); over 20 kg use 300 microgram (EpiPen); over 50 kg use 500 microgram (EpiPen 500). At 20 kg he is at the upper edge of the 150 microgram band, so 150 microgram is correct per current ASCIA paediatric thresholds.
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer first as the primary bronchodilator and observe for response before considering any intramuscular adrenaline dose
options.rationale: Treating anaphylactic wheeze as asthma is the killer anti-pattern; every minute of adrenaline delay increases mortality. Salbutamol-first is acceptable only in isolated bronchospasm without other anaphylaxis features (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines).
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Oral cetirizine and prednisolone with 1-hour observation in the surgery, deferring intramuscular adrenaline pending the antihistamine response
options.rationale: Antihistamines and steroids do not treat airway oedema, bronchospasm or hypotension and have no role as first-line. They are adjuncts after adrenaline, never the initial intervention (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines).
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: IM adrenaline 300 microgram (EpiPen) immediately without weight-checking, accepting the overdose risk in order to avoid any further delay
options.rationale: 300 microgram is the EpiPen dose for over 20 kg under ASCIA; at exactly 20 kg the 150 microgram EpiPen Jr is correct. Would be correct if the child were over 20 kg (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Action Plan).

options.text: Withhold adrenaline as he is talking and responsive; await paramedic arrival before any intramuscular pharmacological intervention is given
options.rationale: Talking and responsive does not exclude anaphylaxis; hoarse voice and throat tightness are airway red flags requiring immediate adrenaline. Withholding while awaiting paramedics is the textbook delay-to-death error (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines).
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis (ASCIA): any acute onset illness with typical skin features PLUS involvement of respiratory and/or cardiovascular and/or persistent severe GI symptoms; or acute onset hypotension/bronchospasm/upper airway obstruction even without skin signs. Treat with IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg) anterolateral mid-thigh, repeat every 5 minutes as needed.
key_points: Adrenaline IM is the only first-line drug
Autoinjector dosing: <7.5 kg consider IM ampoule, 7.5-20 kg = 150 microg, >20 kg = 300 microg
Positioning: supine + legs elevated unless airway compromise or vomiting
Salbutamol, oxygen, IV fluids are adjuncts
Antihistamines and steroids are NOT first-line and do not prevent biphasic reactions
explanation.pearls: If you are debating whether it is anaphylaxis, give the adrenaline. Underuse causes deaths; overuse causes transient palpitations.

sources.label: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

stem: A 10-year-old boy, weight 32 kg, presents to ED 25 minutes after a wasp sting with urticaria, lip swelling, stridor, expiratory wheeze and persistent hypotension. Paramedics gave IM adrenaline 300 microg en route. On arrival to ED 10 minutes later he remained tachypnoeic with stridor, wheeze and BP 78/40, so a second IM adrenaline 300 microg was given into the anterolateral thigh and an IV 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus was commenced. Now 5 minutes after the second IM dose he remains drowsy with BP 82/44, HR 156, RR 40, SpO2 92% on 15 L/min via non-rebreather, with audible stridor and wheeze. Both IM doses were confirmed delivered correctly. The ED consultant is at the bedside, anaesthetics has been called for airway support, and a second IV line has been sited.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 82/44, HR 156, RR 40, SpO2 92% on 15 L NRB.
data_table.Examination: Drowsy. Stridor and expiratory wheeze. Lip and tongue swelling. Mottled peripheries.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Bolus IV adrenaline 1 mg (1:10,000) over 1 minute
options.rationale: 1 mg IV (1:10,000) is the cardiac arrest dose and will cause hypertensive crisis, myocardial ischaemia or VF in a perfusing patient. Would be correct only in cardiac arrest, not in refractory anaphylaxis with a pulse (Source: ASCIA; ARC Paediatric ALS).

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone and IV promethazine
options.rationale: Steroids/antihistamines do nothing for refractory shock and promethazine is contraindicated under 2 years (paradoxical reactions, hypotension). Only ever adjuncts after definitive adrenaline therapy (Source: ASCIA; TGA Promethazine warning).

options.text: Commence IV adrenaline infusion, titrated to response
options.rationale: Refractory anaphylaxis after two correctly delivered IM doses is an indication for IV adrenaline infusion, starting at 0.1 microg/kg/min (range 0.05-1 microg/kg/min) and titrated to BP, perfusion and airway response, while continuing fluid resuscitation. Boluses of IV adrenaline outside cardiac arrest carry significant risk of arrhythmia and hypertensive crisis and are not first-line (ASCIA Refractory Anaphylaxis Guidelines).

options.text: Continue IM adrenaline every 5 minutes only and avoid IV adrenaline
options.rationale: After two failed IM doses, peripheral perfusion is poor and IM absorption unreliable; persisting with IM is the refractory-anaphylaxis pitfall. IV infusion is the next step (Source: ASCIA Refractory Anaphylaxis Guidelines).

options.text: Commence IV glucagon as first-line, no further adrenaline
options.rationale: Glucagon has a narrow niche: beta-blocked patients with refractory shock unresponsive to adrenaline. It is an adjunct, not a replacement for adrenaline infusion (Source: ASCIA Refractory Anaphylaxis Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Refractory anaphylaxis = ongoing features after 2 appropriate IM adrenaline doses. Move to IV adrenaline infusion (0.1 microg/kg/min, titrate), aggressive IV fluids (up to 50 mL/kg crystalloid), prepare for early intubation given airway oedema, consider nebulised adrenaline for upper airway and salbutamol for bronchospasm. Glucagon if on beta-blockers; vasopressin if catecholamine-refractory.
key_points: IV adrenaline infusion: 0.1 microg/kg/min start, titrate 0.05-1
Continue fluid resuscitation 20 mL/kg, repeat to 50 mL/kg
Early airway control - intubation before complete obstruction
Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg for upper airway oedema
Salbutamol for persistent bronchospasm
explanation.pearls: If two correctly placed IM doses have failed, the problem is absorption or severity - not the drug. Move to infusion and call ICU.

subtopic_detail: Refractory anaphylaxis after two IM doses

stem: A 4-year-old girl, weight 16 kg, is brought into ED by ambulance with a generalised tonic-clonic seizure that began at home 12 minutes ago. Paramedics gave buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg 8 minutes after onset, but the seizure has continued. On arrival she is convulsing, with intact airway, no cyanosis, SpO2 96% on 8 L Hudson mask, HR 168, BP 110/70, T 38.1 degC. Bedside glucose is 5.8 mmol/L. She has no known epilepsy, no medications, and no significant past medical history. IV access has just been obtained. There is no preceding head trauma, no toxin exposure and no recent vaccination. She has no congenital heart disease, no metabolic disorder and no family history of epilepsy. Her mother reports a 2-day febrile coryzal illness with mild diarrhoea, treated with paracetamol only. On arrival a nurse-led airway manoeuvre maintains a patent airway with oropharyngeal suction of secretions, the ED consultant is at the bedside and PIPER retrieval has been alerted to assist with potential PICU transfer.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.1 degC, HR 168, RR breaths irregular, SpO2 96% on 8 L Hudson, BP 110/70.
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic activity ongoing. Airway patent with jaw thrust. No focal lateralising features evident.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.8 mmol/L. No bloods yet.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next medication?

options.text: Repeat buccal midazolam now
options.rationale: Re-dosing buccal once IV is in place wastes a more reliable route and risks under-dosing through ongoing buccal pooling. Buccal would be the right second dose only if IV access were still unavailable (Source: RCH CPG: Status Epilepticus).

options.text: IV phenytoin load over 20 min
options.rationale: Phenytoin/levetiracetam is the second-line step after two adequate benzodiazepine doses; only one has been given. Would be correct if two benzodiazepine doses had already failed (Source: RCH CPG: Status Epilepticus).

options.text: IV phenobarbital load now
options.rationale: Phenobarbital is reserved for refractory status after benzodiazepines plus a second-line agent have failed; jumping straight to it skips the standard ladder. Would be the right step in established refractory status (Source: APLS Australia).

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation with thiopentone, rocuronium
options.rationale: RSI with thiopentone is the refractory-status escalation only after benzodiazepines and at least one second-line agent fail, with airway/breathing currently maintained the patient should not be intubated yet. Would be correct in refractory status with airway compromise (Source: APLS Australia).

options.text: IV midazolam bolus now
options.rationale: Per APLS Australia and RCH status epilepticus pathway, after one benzodiazepine that has failed (and 10 minutes have elapsed), the second-line is a SECOND benzodiazepine dose - IV midazolam 0.15 mg/kg (or IV diazepam 0.25 mg/kg) - now that IV access is available. If that fails after a further 10 minutes, move to a second-line agent such as levetiracetam 40 mg/kg or phenytoin 20 mg/kg (RCH CPG; APLS).

explanation.summary: APLS status epilepticus pathway: 0 min - ABC, oxygen, check glucose. 5 min - first benzodiazepine (IV midazolam/diazepam or buccal/IM midazolam). 15 min - second benzodiazepine. 25 min - second-line: levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg IV over 5 min OR phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV over 20 min. 45 min - RSI with thiopentone, transfer to PICU.
key_points: First and second benzodiazepine before moving to long-acting agent
Levetiracetam now preferred over phenytoin in many centres (faster, fewer arrhythmias, EcLiPSE/ConSEPT trials)
Check and correct glucose, sodium, calcium, magnesium
Pyridoxine 100 mg IV if under 18 months and refractory
Get help early - status >30 minutes risks neurological injury
explanation.pearls: Most paediatric status responds to two doses of benzodiazepine. If you are about to give a third benzodiazepine, you are wrong - move on.

stem: A previously well 5-year-old boy, weight 19 kg, is brought to ED after a witnessed event at home in which he became suddenly unresponsive, stiffened, then had rhythmic jerking of all four limbs for about 90 seconds, followed by 10 minutes of confusion and sleepiness. He has now fully recovered. He has had no fever, no head injury, and no family history of epilepsy. Birth and developmental history are normal and he is fully immunised. On examination he is alert, neurologically intact with no focal deficit, no rash, normal fundi, and a normal cardiovascular exam. Bedside glucose is 4.9 mmol/L. Sodium, calcium and magnesium are normal. He has no further seizures during 4 hours of ED observation. His mother reports no preceding head injury, no toxic ingestion, and no recent travel. He attends mainstream kindergarten where he is meeting expected milestones, and there is no family history of epilepsy or sudden cardiac death.

data_table.Examination: GCS 15. Normal cranial nerves, tone, power, reflexes, coordination, gait. No rash. No meningism.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 4.9, Na 139, K 4.1, Ca 2.35, Mg 0.85, FBC normal, CRP <5.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Discharge with outpatient EEG in 1-2 weeks, safety advice, no anti-epileptic medication
options.rationale: After a first unprovoked generalised tonic-clonic seizure in a neurologically normal child, the standard approach is outpatient first-fit clinic review with EEG within 1-2 weeks (which informs recurrence risk), MRI only if there are focal features or under 2 years or refractory, no anti-epileptic medication after a single seizure unless recurrence risk is high, and safety counselling (no baths alone, supervised swimming, no high climbing). Parental driving is not restricted (RCH CPG Afebrile seizures).

options.text: Admit and commence sodium valproate daily, refer neurology
options.rationale: Starting an antiepileptic after a single unprovoked seizure is the textbook over-treatment error; recurrence is around 40 percent and early AED does not improve long-term outcome. Would be considered only after a second seizure or with high-risk EEG/MRI findings (Source: RCH CPG: First Seizure; NICE NG217).

options.text: Urgent inpatient CT head and lumbar puncture now before any discharge
options.rationale: CT plus LP for a brief generalised seizure with full recovery is the over-investigation anti-pattern: no fever, no meningism, no focal deficit, no trauma. Indicated only with persistent altered GCS, focal deficit, suspected meningitis, or trauma (Source: RCH CPG: First Seizure).

options.text: Discharge with buccal midazolam rescue kit, no follow-up unless seizures recur
options.rationale: A rescue kit is not standard after a single brief seizure; first-fit pathway is EEG-led follow-up, not pre-emptive home benzodiazepines. Would be appropriate only for established epilepsy or known prolonged seizures over 5 minutes (Source: RCH CPG: First Seizure).

options.text: Discharge with same-day MRI brain and start levetiracetam if MRI abnormal
options.rationale: Same-day MRI is over-imaging for a generalised seizure with normal exam; outpatient MRI is the standard timing. Urgent MRI is reserved for focal seizures, persistent deficit, age under 2 years, or refractory seizures (Source: RCH CPG: First Seizure).

explanation.summary: First unprovoked afebrile seizure in an otherwise well child: ABC, exclude provokers (glucose, electrolytes, toxins, infection, trauma), full neurological examination. If recovered and normal: discharge with first-fit clinic referral, EEG within 1-2 weeks, MRI only for focal features/under 2/refractory, safety advice, no AED. Recurrence risk approximately 40%; epilepsy is diagnosed after 2 unprovoked seizures.
key_points: EEG within 1-2 weeks of first seizure characterises syndrome
MRI for: focal seizure, under 2 years, persistent deficit, refractory
AEDs not started after single seizure (no change in long-term outcome)
Safety: baths supervised, no swimming alone, no high climbing
Australian driving rules apply to the patient (>16) not the parents
explanation.pearls: First-fit clinics exist for a reason - rushing to imaging and starting medication after one event is overinvestigation and overtreatment.

subtopic_detail: First non-febrile seizure - investigations and counselling

stem: A previously well 18-month-old boy weighing 11 kg presents to ED after a single 3-minute generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home. He had been febrile to 39.0 deg C with coryzal symptoms for 24 hours. He recovered fully within 20 minutes. On examination he is alert and playful with a clear viral URTI and no other source of infection. There is no rash, no meningism and no focal neurology. He has no past seizure history; his father had two febrile seizures as a toddler. He is fully immunised, has not received sedating medication and has no known allergies. Bedside glucose 5.2. T 38.7, HR 138, RR 28, SpO2 99 percent.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7 degC, HR 138, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Alert, playful. Coryza. No rash, no meningism, no focal signs. Anterior fontanelle closed.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.2 mmol/L. No further investigations performed.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Admit overnight for inpatient EEG and brain MRI to investigate the seizure aetiology before considering any discharge from the emergency department
options.rationale: EEG and MRI are not indicated after a simple febrile seizure with full recovery and normal exam; this is the febrile-seizure over-investigation anti-pattern. Would be appropriate only for complex febrile seizures (focal, prolonged over 15 minutes, or recurrent within 24 hours) (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Seizure).

options.text: Lumbar puncture before discharge given the age under 2 years, as part of routine post-febrile-seizure workup at this age
options.rationale: Routine LP for age under 2 is the legacy rule that AU guidelines have abandoned; a well, fully immunised child without meningism or persistent altered conscious state does not need CSF. Would be indicated only with meningism, persistent drowsiness, or incomplete Hib/pneumococcal vaccination (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Seizure).

options.text: Regular alternating paracetamol and ibuprofen at home to prevent further febrile seizure recurrence over the next 24-48 hours of this illness
options.rationale: Scheduled antipyretics do not reduce recurrence and may delay recognition of further illness; use for comfort only. Would never be correct as a prophylactic strategy (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Seizure).

options.text: Reassurance, treat the febrile focus per eTG, discharge with written safety-net advice; advise recurrence risk is around 30% and epilepsy risk 1-2%
options.rationale: This is a simple febrile seizure (single, generalised, under 15 minutes, full recovery, age 6 months to 6 years). Management is reassurance, treatment of the febrile focus, written first-aid advice and routine follow-up; no investigations are needed and no antiepileptic is indicated.

options.text: Prescribe maintenance sodium valproate at discharge to prevent further febrile seizures, with paediatric neurology follow-up arranged in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Maintenance AEDs are not used for simple febrile seizures regardless of recurrence rate; risks outweigh benefit. Considered only for very prolonged or frequent complex febrile seizures under specialist review (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Seizure).

explanation.summary: Simple febrile seizure: age 6 months to 5 years, generalised, <15 minutes, single in 24 hours, full recovery, with fever and no CNS infection. Management: identify and treat the febrile illness, reassurance, safety advice, no investigations beyond what the source illness requires, no anti-epileptic, antipyretics for comfort only.
key_points: Complex = focal OR >15 min OR >1 in 24 h
Recurrence risk approximately 30% overall; higher if first seizure <18 months or family history
Subsequent epilepsy risk approximately 1-2% (background 0.5-1%)
Antipyretics do NOT prevent recurrence
LP only if meningism, age <6 months, or incomplete recovery
explanation.pearls: Parents are usually traumatised by their first febrile seizure - the most therapeutic intervention is unhurried explanation that this is benign.

stem: A 4-year-old boy, weight 18 kg, presents with 3 days of high fever and a 1-day history of vomiting and reduced intake. He has no chronic illness. On examination he is irritable but rousable to voice, tachycardic, with mottled knees, cool hands and feet, central capillary refill of 4 seconds, peripheral pulses present but thready, and a non-tender abdomen with no rash. His HR is 168, RR 36, T 39.4 degC, SpO2 97% on room air, and his non-invasive BP is 96/64 mmHg. He has not voided in 8 hours. Bedside glucose is 4.4 mmol/L, venous lactate 3.6 mmol/L. IV access is being established. He has not received antibiotics in the last 3 months and his vaccinations including pneumococcal and meningococcal B are up to date. The senior nurse has activated the local sepsis pathway and broad-spectrum antibiotics are being drawn up while access is established.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 degC, HR 168, RR 36, BP 96/64, SpO2 97% RA, cap refill 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Irritable, rousable. Mottled, cool peripheries. Thready pulses. Soft abdomen. No rash.

lead_in: Which best describes his haemodynamic state and the immediate management?

options.text: Cardiogenic shock; restrict fluid and commence IV dobutamine infusion
options.rationale: No cardiac history, gallop, hepatomegaly or pulmonary crackles to suggest pump failure; mislabelling sepsis as cardiogenic delays fluid resuscitation. Would be correct with known cardiomyopathy, post-cardiac-surgery, or signs of failure (Source: APLS Australia).

options.text: Decompensated shock; commence adrenaline infusion immediately before any fluid
options.rationale: BP is preserved so by definition this is compensated shock; jumping to adrenaline before fluid breaks the resuscitation order. Vasoactives are added only once 40-60 mL/kg of crystalloid has failed (Source: APLS Australia; Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric 2020).

options.text: No shock; give maintenance fluid only and observe
options.rationale: Cap refill 4 s, mottling, oliguria and lactate 3.6 is not normal; calling this 'no shock' is the well-looking-but-decompensating trap. Maintenance-only would be correct with normal perfusion, vitals and lactate (Source: APLS Australia).

options.text: Warm vasodilatory shock; give noradrenaline infusion as first-line
options.rationale: Warm shock shows flash cap refill, bounding pulses and wide pulse pressure; this child has the opposite (cold shock). Noradrenaline-first would be appropriate in fluid-refractory warm septic shock, not cold (Source: APLS Australia; Surviving Sepsis Paediatric 2020).

options.text: Compensated cold shock; give 10-20 mL/kg saline bolus
options.rationale: Tachycardia disproportionate to fever, prolonged capillary refill, cool mottled peripheries, oliguria and an elevated lactate define compensated cold shock with maintained BP. The first-line intervention is a 10-20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid bolus over 5-10 minutes with reassessment after each bolus, alongside broad-spectrum antibiotics if sepsis is suspected (APLS Australia; RCH CPG Sepsis).

explanation.summary: Children compensate well by raising heart rate and systemic vascular resistance; hypotension is a late and pre-arrest sign. Recognise compensated shock from tachycardia disproportionate to fever, prolonged capillary refill, mottling, cool peripheries, altered mental state, and oliguria, regardless of BP. Treat with 10-20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid boluses with reassessment after each.
key_points: Hypotension is a late sign in children - do not wait for it
Cold shock: cool peripheries, prolonged CR, narrow pulse pressure - first-line adrenaline if fluid-refractory
Warm shock: flash CR, wide pulse pressure - first-line noradrenaline if fluid-refractory
Bolus 10-20 mL/kg over 5-10 minutes; reassess after each
Stop fluid if signs of overload (hepatomegaly, crackles)
explanation.pearls: A normal BP in a sick child is reassurance you don't deserve. Look at the periphery, the lactate and the mental state.

stem: A 6-year-old girl, weight 22 kg, was brought in shocked with presumed septic source (community-acquired pneumonia with a parapneumonic effusion on bedside lung ultrasound). She received empirical IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg early in the resuscitation. Over the last 40 minutes she has received three 20 mL/kg boluses of 0.9% saline (total 60 mL/kg) and remains tachycardic at 156, with central capillary refill of 4 seconds, cool mottled peripheries, BP 78/40 mmHg, RR 38, SpO2 94% on 8 L Hudson mask. She has not voided since arrival. The hepatic edge is now palpable 1 cm below the costal margin and there are bibasal crackles. Lactate has fallen from 5.4 to 3.8 mmol/L. The retrieval service is on the phone and the paediatric registrar is preparing for central access. Her mother reports a 3-day febrile illness with cough and lethargy treated by the GP with oral amoxicillin 48 hours ago without improvement. The bedside echocardiogram clip the registrar has just performed shows a hyperdynamic but underfilled left ventricle with no pericardial effusion.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, BP 78/40, RR 38, SpO2 94% on 8 L, cap refill 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Cool mottled peripheries, narrow pulse pressure. Hepatomegaly 1 cm. Bibasal crackles. Anuric.
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 3.8 mmol/L (was 5.4). VBG pH 7.28. CXR: right lower lobe consolidation with small effusion.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence peripheral adrenaline infusion and prepare for intubation
options.rationale: She has fluid-refractory septic shock with cold-shock physiology (narrow pulse pressure, mottled cool peripheries, prolonged CR) and now signs of fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles). Australian guidelines support starting adrenaline as first-line inotrope in paediatric cold septic shock, and peripheral infusion at low-dose concentrations (0.05-0.3 microg/kg/min) is acceptable while central access is obtained. Further large-volume fluid boluses risk worsening overload (ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis; APLS; Surviving Sepsis Campaign paediatric 2020).

options.text: Give a fourth 20 mL/kg saline bolus before any vasoactive medication
options.rationale: Hepatomegaly and crackles signal volume overload; further large boluses worsen pulmonary oedema and hypoxaemia. This is the fluid-overload paediatric-sepsis trap; reassess after 40-60 mL/kg and switch to inotrope (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric 2020).

options.text: Start noradrenaline as first-line inotrope peripherally
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is the warm-shock first-line; cold shock with poor cardiac output is treated with adrenaline. Would be correct in fluid-refractory shock with wide pulse pressure and warm peripheries (Source: APLS Australia).

options.text: Commence dopamine infusion peripherally as first-line
options.rationale: Dopamine showed higher mortality than adrenaline in the Ventura 2015 paediatric RCT and is no longer first-line. Considered only when adrenaline/noradrenaline unavailable (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric 2020).

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone as next step, no inotrope
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is an adjunct for catecholamine-resistant shock or proven/suspected adrenal insufficiency, not a substitute for inotropes. Would be added on top of adrenaline if shock persists despite escalating doses (Source: Surviving Sepsis Paediatric 2020).

explanation.summary: Fluid-refractory shock after 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid requires vasoactive support. Choose adrenaline for cold shock (low cardiac output, high SVR) and noradrenaline for warm shock (high cardiac output, low SVR). Peripheral low-dose infusion is acceptable while central access is established. Watch for fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles, S3, increasing oxygen requirement) and stop boluses if present.
key_points: Fluid-refractory = persistent shock after 40-60 mL/kg
Cold shock -> adrenaline 0.05-0.3 microg/kg/min
Warm shock -> noradrenaline 0.05-0.3 microg/kg/min
Peripheral infusion safe at low concentrations until central access
Hydrocortisone 50 mg/m2/day or 2 mg/kg if catecholamine-resistant
explanation.pearls: If you can feel the liver, you can stop the fluid. Paediatric fluid overload is iatrogenic and worsens outcomes.

stem: A 4-month-old, ex-term, breastfed boy is brought to ED by his mother because of unsettled crying for the last 12 hours. She mentions that she noticed two bruises on his cheek and a bruise on his left ear earlier in the day, which she attributes to him knocking himself on a toy in the cot. He has not yet rolled or crawled and is not pulling himself up. There is no preceding illness and no fever. He is fully immunised and has had routine well-child checks with no concerns. Examination reveals a well-grown alert infant with a 2 cm bruise to the right cheek, a 1 cm bruise to the left pinna and a 1.5 cm bruise on the left frontal scalp, no other external injuries, a normal anterior fontanelle, no focal neurology, no retinal haemorrhages on examination by the registrar, normal tone, and a normal abdomen with no organomegaly. Coagulation screen, FBC and LFTs are normal. The mother is alone and tearful when asked about who else cares for the baby.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 degC, HR 142, RR 36, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Three bruises (cheek, ear, frontal scalp). Otherwise unremarkable. Pre-mobile.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, coags, LFTs normal. No CT or skeletal survey yet.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Send for outpatient coagulation studies only and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Normal coags do not exclude abuse, and a bleeding disorder cannot account for patterned bruising in a pre-mobile infant. Outpatient coags alone would be appropriate only if there were no concerning history/pattern and an isolated bruise with positive family bleeding history (Source: RCH CPG: Non-Accidental Injury).

options.text: Reassure the mother and discharge with GP follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Bruises in a pre-mobile infant are sentinel injuries: 'those who don't cruise rarely bruise.' Reassure-and-discharge is the textbook safeguarding failure. Reassurance is appropriate only after full workup is reassuring (Source: RCH CPG: Non-Accidental Injury).

options.text: Admit for skeletal survey, CT head, ophthalmology review, child protection consult and mandatory report
options.rationale: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant meets the TEN-4 FACESp rule (Torso, Ear, Neck, age <=4 months; Frenulum, Angle of jaw, Cheek, Eyelid, Sclera, patterned) and is a strong red flag for inflicted injury. This child has all four red-flag locations triggered. Mandatory next steps are full skeletal survey (and repeat at 2 weeks), non-contrast CT head, dilated ophthalmology examination for retinal haemorrhages, paediatric team and child protection consultation, and mandatory notification to the state child protection authority. Doctors are mandated reporters in every Australian state and territory (RCH CPG Child abuse; AAP TEN-4 FACES).
source_refs: Pierce et al. TEN-4 FACESp validation 2021

options.text: Order a skeletal survey only, without notifying child protection, pending the imaging result
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting is triggered by reasonable suspicion, not confirmed imaging; delaying notification breaches the statutory duty under state Children and Young Persons Acts. Would never be correct to defer notification pending results (Source: NSW/Vic mandatory reporter legislation; RCH CPG).

options.text: Confront the mother directly about possible abuse before doing any investigations
options.rationale: Direct confrontation by the treating team can compromise the child's safety, the forensic investigation and ongoing therapeutic relationships; that role belongs to child protection and police. Investigation and notification proceed without confrontation (Source: RCH CPG: Non-Accidental Injury).

explanation.summary: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant is the most specific sign of physical abuse in early childhood. Apply the TEN-4 FACESp rule: bruises in any of these regions or in a child <=4 months are highly suspicious. Investigations: full skeletal survey (with 2-week repeat), CT head, dilated fundus exam, coagulation screen. Mandatory reporting in all Australian jurisdictions for doctors with reasonable suspicion.
key_points: Those who don't cruise rarely bruise
TEN-4: Torso, Ear, Neck, age 4 months and under
FACESp: Frenulum, Angle of jaw, Cheek, Eyelid, Sclera, patterned
Skeletal survey at presentation and repeat at 2 weeks (occult fractures)
Mandatory reporting is by suspicion, not certainty - this is statutory
explanation.pearls: Document objectively, do not investigate the social history beyond what is clinically necessary, and let the multidisciplinary team and statutory agency lead the safeguarding process.

sources.label: Pierce et al. TEN-4 FACESp validation 2021
AIFS Mandatory reporting of child abuse

stem: A 4-year-old girl, weight 16 kg, was pulled from a backyard pool by her father approximately 6 minutes ago after an unwitnessed submersion estimated at 3-5 minutes. She was apnoeic and unresponsive at poolside. Her father began chest compressions immediately and bystander CPR continued until paramedics arrived 4 minutes ago. Paramedics found her in asystole, established a supraglottic airway, ventilated with 100% oxygen and continued compressions; ROSC was achieved after 3 minutes of resuscitation. She arrives in ED still intubated (now endotracheal), with HR 132, BP 86/52, SpO2 92% on 100% FiO2, T 33.4 degC tympanic, GCS 3 with no sedation, pupils 4 mm sluggish bilaterally. Bedside lung ultrasound shows bilateral B-lines and the CXR is in progress. An indwelling urinary catheter has drained 20 mL of clear urine since insertion 15 minutes ago, and the PICU retrieval team has been activated.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, BP 86/52, SpO2 92% on FiO2 1.0, T 33.4 degC, GCS 3.
data_table.Examination: Intubated and ventilated. Bilateral crackles. Cool peripheries. Pupils 4 mm, sluggish.
data_table.Investigations: Lung US: bilateral B-lines. VBG pending. Bedside glucose 7.1 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which best reflects current ANZCOR-aligned management priorities for this child?

options.text: Withdraw care now given asystole at scene; outcome is uniformly poor
options.rationale: Paediatric drowning with ROSC and short submersion has documented good neuro outcomes; early withdrawal is the prognostic-nihilism error. Withdrawal discussion is appropriate only after 48-72 h of intensive care and clear secondary-survey/imaging confirmation of devastating injury (Source: ANZCOR Drowning).
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim

options.text: Hyperventilate to PaCO2 25 mmHg and target SpO2 100% on FiO2 1.0
options.rationale: Hyperoxia and hypocapnia cause cerebral vasoconstriction and oxidative reperfusion injury post-arrest; target normoxia and normocapnia. Hyperventilation is acceptable only for transient management of impending herniation (Source: ANZCOR Post-Resuscitation Care).
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 12 Paediatric advanced life support

options.text: Aggressive rapid rewarming to 38 degC plus prophylactic broad-spectrum IV ceftriaxone now
options.rationale: Induced hyperthermia worsens neurological outcomes; controlled rewarming to 36 degC is the standard. Empirical antibiotics are reserved for documented aspiration of contaminated water or clinical evidence of infection (Source: ANZCOR; RCH CPG Drowning).
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim

options.text: Early extubation because the lungs are clear on auscultation and CXR is unremarkable
options.rationale: Drowning-related pulmonary oedema and ARDS can be delayed up to 8 hours; clear CXR does not justify early extubation when GCS is low and B-lines are present. Extubation is appropriate only with normal gas exchange, GCS over 12 and resolved B-lines (Source: ANZCOR).
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim

options.text: Lung-protective ventilation with PEEP, controlled rewarming to 36 degC, post-arrest care, discuss ECMO retrieval
options.rationale: Post-drowning care follows ANZCOR resuscitation principles plus post-cardiac-arrest care. Five initial rescue breaths precede compressions in drowning because hypoxia is the proximate cause. After ROSC, target normoxia (SpO2 94-98%), normocapnia, lung-protective ventilation with PEEP for pulmonary oedema/ARDS, treat shock with fluids and adrenaline, and discuss VA-ECMO for refractory hypoxia or cardiac dysfunction. Rewarm to 36 degC at 0.5-1 degC/h (do not over-rewarm; mild controlled hypothermia is no longer routinely targeted but normothermia is). Antibiotics are not routine.
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim
ANZCOR Guideline 12 Paediatric advanced life support

explanation.summary: Drowning is primarily an asphyxial injury - prioritise oxygenation. Bystander BLS for drowning: 5 rescue breaths first, then 30:2 CPR (lay rescuer) or 15:2 (two trained rescuers in a child). Post-ROSC: lung-protective ventilation with PEEP for pulmonary oedema/ARDS, normoxia, normocapnia, treat shock, rewarm to 36 degC, manage as post-cardiac-arrest syndrome. Consider VA-ECMO for refractory hypoxia or cardiac dysfunction. Late ARDS can develop up to 8 hours later.
key_points: 5 rescue breaths first in drowning (not compressions first)
Target SpO2 94-98% post-ROSC; avoid hyperoxia and hypocapnia
PEEP for pulmonary oedema/ARDS; lung-protective tidal volumes
Rewarm to 36 degC at 0.5-1 degC/h; avoid hyperthermia
Antibiotics not routine; consider if contaminated water or sepsis develops
explanation.pearls: The lungs of a drowned child can look normal at 1 hour and ARDS by 8 hours. Do not extubate to room air based on the initial CXR.

sources.label: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim
ANZCOR Guideline 12 Paediatric advanced life support

stem: A 3-year-old girl, weight 15 kg, sustained a scald injury 90 minutes ago when she pulled a pot of hot water onto herself. On assessment in ED, she has partial-thickness burns to the anterior chest, anterior abdomen and the front of both thighs, with sparing of the face, hands, perineum and circumferential injuries. Using a Lund-Browder chart for her age, the total burn surface area (TBSA) is calculated at 18%. She is alert, in pain (Faces score 8/10), HR 148, BP 96/60, RR 32, SpO2 99% on room air, T 36.4 degC. IV access has been obtained and tetanus status is up to date. She last ate 4 hours ago and has no known allergies. She has no underlying skin condition, no chronic illnessies. The burn was cooled with running tap water for 20 minutes at the scene by an attending neighbour who is a nurse, and the wounds have been covered with cling film during transport.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 148, BP 96/60, RR 32, SpO2 99% RA, T 36.4 degC.
data_table.Examination: Partial-thickness burns anterior chest, abdomen, anterior thighs. 18% TBSA. Mucosae moist. Peripheral perfusion adequate.
data_table.Investigations: VBG pending. No carboxyhaemoglobin elevation (no inhalation injury suspected).

options.text: 5% albumin 20 mL/kg bolus immediately then 1 mL/kg/%TBSA over the next 24 hours
options.rationale: Colloid worsens lung water in the early capillary-leak phase of burn injury; crystalloid is mandated in the first 24 h. Albumin is added only in the second 24 h or as rescue in failed resuscitation (Source: ANZBA EMSB).
source_refs: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns (EMSB)

options.text: Parkland: 4 mL/kg/%TBSA 0.9% saline = 1080 mL over 24 hours INSTEAD OF maintenance
options.rationale: Two errors: omitting maintenance (children always need maintenance plus burn fluid) and using 0.9 percent saline (hyperchloraemic acidosis risk). Parkland-only would be acceptable in adults; Hartmann's plus separate maintenance is the paediatric standard (Source: RCH CPG: Burns; ANZBA).

options.text: Hypertonic 3% saline at 3 mL/kg/hour for 24 hours, no maintenance separately
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is not standard in paediatric burn resuscitation in Australia and is studied only in specific adult settings. Would never be first-line in children (Source: ANZBA EMSB).
source_refs: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns (EMSB)

options.text: Hartmann's 3 mL/kg/%TBSA over 24 hours (half in first 8 hours) plus separate maintenance
options.rationale: Australian paediatric burns centres use a modified Parkland formula at 3-4 mL/kg/%TBSA of Hartmann's (compound sodium lactate) over the first 24 hours from the time of burn, half in the first 8 hours and half in the next 16 hours, with paediatric maintenance fluid containing glucose given IN ADDITION because children have lower glycogen stores and are at risk of hypoglycaemia. 3 mL x 15 kg x 18 = 810 mL resuscitation fluid (RCH CPG Burns; ANZBA EMSB).
source_refs: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns (EMSB)

options.text: Maintenance fluid only (60 mL/h of 0.9% saline plus dextrose) with no resuscitation fluid
options.rationale: TBSA over 10 percent in children mandates formal burn resuscitation fluid in addition to maintenance; an 18 percent burn cannot be managed with maintenance alone. Maintenance-only is appropriate only for under-10-percent TBSA (Source: RCH CPG: Burns).

explanation.summary: Paediatric burns >=10% TBSA need formal resuscitation. Use modified Parkland: 3-4 mL/kg/%TBSA Hartmann's over 24 hours from time of burn, half in first 8 h, half in next 16 h. Give maintenance dextrose-containing fluid IN ADDITION (children risk hypoglycaemia). Use Lund-Browder chart (not rule of 9s) for paediatric TBSA. Titrate fluid to urine output 1 mL/kg/h. Analgesia (IV morphine or intranasal fentanyl), tetanus, cover with cling film, refer to burns service.
key_points: Modified Parkland 3-4 mL/kg/%TBSA Hartmann's over 24 h
Maintenance with glucose IN ADDITION in children
Lund-Browder chart for TBSA in children
Target urine output 1 mL/kg/h
Refer >10% TBSA, face/hands/perineum, full-thickness, electrical, chemical, suspected NAI
explanation.pearls: Children dehydrate and become hypoglycaemic faster than adults - never run a burned child without glucose-containing maintenance.

sources.label: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns (EMSB)

stem: A 15-year-old girl, weight 60 kg, is brought to ED by her mother 5 hours after ingesting an unknown number of paracetamol 500 mg tablets in an acute single ingestion. She is now tearful but cooperative, denies co-ingestion, has no abdominal pain, no jaundice, and a soft non-tender abdomen. She has no chronic liver disease and is not on enzyme-inducing medications. Vital signs are normal. Bloods drawn 4 hours and 30 minutes post-ingestion show paracetamol concentration 165 mg/L, ALT 28 U/L, INR 1.0, creatinine 58 micromol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L, glucose 5.0 mmol/L, and a negative beta-hCG. The empty packet retrieved at home suggests she may have taken approximately 14 g (around 230 mg/kg) but the ingestion history is uncertain., after which the line is extended to 24 hours.

data_table.Examination: Alert, tearful. No jaundice. Soft non-tender abdomen.
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol 165 mg/L at 4.5 h. ALT 28. INR 1.0. Creatinine 58. Bicarbonate 24. Lactate 1.4. Glucose 5.0. beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat paracetamol level at 8 hours and only commence NAC if above 75 mg/L
options.rationale: Delaying NAC when the level is already above the nomogram treatment line costs hepatoprotective efficacy (best within 8 h of ingestion). Repeat-and-wait would be reasonable only for an asymptomatic patient with a sub-line level and uncertain timing (Source: Australian PIC Paracetamol Guideline).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)

options.text: IV N-acetylcysteine two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h) and admit
options.rationale: At 4.5 hours post-ingestion the paracetamol concentration of 165 mg/L lies above the Australian 150 mg/L treatment line on the modified Rumack-Matthew nomogram (extrapolated back to 4 h would be above 150 mg/L). The reported dose also exceeds the 200 mg/kg supratherapeutic threshold. NAC is indicated. The Australian Poisons Information Centre and AMH endorse the two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h) which reduces anaphylactoid reactions versus the traditional three-bag regimen. Psychiatric assessment is required after any deliberate self-poisoning (AMH; Australian PIC; Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology and Wilderness).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)

options.text: Give oral activated charcoal 1 g/kg now and reassess level at 12 hours
options.rationale: Activated charcoal needs to be within 1-2 h (or up to 4 h for sustained-release); at 5 h post-ingestion it is not useful and must never delay NAC. Would be appropriate within 1-2 hours of ingestion (Source: Australian PIC).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)

options.text: Discharge with mental health follow-up only; level is below treatment line and dose under 200 mg/kg
options.rationale: 165 mg/L at 4.5 h is above the 150 mg/L treatment line, and 230 mg/kg exceeds the 200 mg/kg dose threshold; the higher of dose-or-level governs. Would be appropriate only if BOTH dose under 200 mg/kg AND level below line (Source: Australian PIC).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)

options.text: Commence oral methionine and arrange urgent liver transplant assessment at tertiary centre now
options.rationale: Oral methionine is not used in Australia and transplant assessment is for established fulminant failure meeting King's College criteria, not initial presentation. NAC is the standard antidote (Source: AMH; Australian PIC).

explanation.summary: Acute single paracetamol ingestion: take a 4-hour-or-later level and plot on the Australian nomogram with a single treatment line starting at 150 mg/L at 4 h. NAC indicated if level above line, OR dose >200 mg/kg (or >10 g) with no level available within 8 hours, OR staggered/repeated supratherapeutic ingestion, OR ALT elevated. Use the two-bag IV NAC regimen: 200 mg/kg over 4 h then 100 mg/kg over 16 h. After deliberate self-poisoning, mental health assessment is mandatory.
key_points: Australian nomogram: single 150 mg/L line at 4 h, log-linear to 24 h
Two-bag NAC: 200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h (fewer reactions vs 3-bag)
Supratherapeutic: >200 mg/kg or >10 g acute
Charcoal useful only within 1-2 h (up to 4 h for SR)
Repeat ALT, INR, paracetamol at end of NAC; continue NAC if ALT rising or INR >1.3
explanation.pearls: Never withhold NAC waiting for a perfect history. When dose, level and time are uncertain, treat - hepatotoxicity is the unrecoverable error.

sources.label: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)
